header.label: Basic Infrastructure
header.headline: Power, water, toilets — the basics that aren't basic yet · 2020-21
header.sub: 15.1 L schools. Electricity: 84%, Drinking Water: 98%, Toilet: 98%

cards.label: Electricity
cards.note: 84% of schools have electricity. Govt: 82%, Private: 91%. 2.0 Cr. students study in schools without electricity.

cards.label: Drinking Water
cards.note: 98% of schools have drinking water. Govt: 97%, Private: 99%. 0.3 Cr. students in schools without drinking water.

cards.label: Toilet
cards.note: 98% of schools have functional toilets. Govt: 99%, Private: 100%. 0.1 Cr. students in schools without toilets.

cards.label: Tinkering Lab
cards.note: 1% of schools have a tinkering lab. Govt: 0%, Private: 4%. 1.4 Cr. students have access to tinkering facilities.

basic.mgmtSource: Central Govt schools lead all basic indicators. Government schools lag Private Unaided by ~11pts on electricity access — a key equity gap to close.
basic.mgmtNote: Central Govt schools lead all four indicators. Government schools lag Private Unaided by ~11pts on electricity. From Management column in basic_infra_overall.
basic.catSource: Secondary schools have the highest basic infrastructure coverage. Foundational and Preparatory stages — serving the largest enrollment — show persistent gaps in electricity and tinkering labs.
basic.catNote: Foundational & Prep schools have the lowest coverage on every indicator — they serve the youngest and most numerous students.

insight1.label: Students in Schools Without a Toilet · 2020-21
insight1.note: total_students. Largest student-impact gap of any basic indicator. 2% of schools lack a toilet — concentrated in Foundational & Prep, government-managed schools

insight2.label: Schools with All 3 Basic Facilities · 2020-21
insight2.note: 83%. 17% of schools — 2.6 L — are missing at least one basic facility
insight2.delta_label: Schools missing at least one basic facility

header.label: CWSN Infrastructure
header.headline: Accessibility infrastructure for children with special needs
header.sub: 15.1 L schools, 0.2 Cr CWSN students. Ramps: 71%, Both ramps+handrails: 48%. From ramps_yn, handrails_yn, toiletb/g_cwsn_fun per udise_sch_code

cards.label: Ramps
cards.note: 71%. Govt: 82%, Private: 47%. 4.4 L schools lack ramps

cards.label: Handrails
cards.note: 48%. Govt: 57%, Private: 30%. 7.8 L schools lack handrails

cards.label: CWSN Boy Toilet
cards.note: 20%. Govt: 18%, Private: 26%. 12.0 L schools lack cwsn boy toilet

cards.label: CWSN Girl Toilet
cards.note: 19%. Govt: 17%, Private: 26%. 12.2 L schools lack cwsn girl toilet

cwsn.mgmtSource: CWSN accessibility infrastructure is highest in Central Govt schools. State Govt schools, which enroll 78% of CWSN students, show notable gaps in ramps and functional toilets.
cwsn.mgmtNote: Central Govt schools lead on ramps and handrails. Govt schools lag private on all CWSN indicators. From Management column in cwsn_infra_overall.
cwsn.catSource: Secondary schools lead on CWSN infrastructure compliance. Foundational schools, which serve the youngest CWSN children, have the lowest ramp and accessible toilet coverage.
cwsn.catNote: Secondary schools have the highest CWSN facility coverage. Foundational & Prep schools — where many younger CWSN students study — have the least.

insight1.label: Schools with Both Ramps & Handrails · 2020-21
insight1.note: 48%. Full physical access requires both. 7.8 L schools lack at least one

insight2.label: Schools with No Ramp · 2020-21
insight2.note: Ramps are the most basic physical access requirement — yet 29% of schools still lack them, locking out wheelchair users

header.label: Digital Infrastructure
header.headline: Connectivity, computers, broadband — the digital divide · 2020-21
header.sub: 15.1 L schools. Internet: 25%, Computers: 34%, Broadband: 0%. Total computers: 5.3M. From internet_yn, computer_yn, broadband_yn, tot_computer

cards.label: Internet
cards.note: 25%. Govt: 14%, Private: 53%

cards.label: Computers
cards.note: 34%. Total computers in system: 5.3M. Avg 10.1 computers per equipped school

cards.label: Broadband
cards.note: 0%. Govt: 0%, Private: 0%

cards.label: Comp Lab (≥5 PCs)
cards.note: 34%. Avg computers per equipped school = 10.1. 15.6 Cr

digital.mgmtSource: Digital infrastructure adoption is led by Government Aided schools for internet connectivity. Private Unaided schools lead on computer availability but lag on broadband quality.
digital.mgmtNote: Central Govt and Private Unaided schools lead on all digital indicators. Government schools show largest gap on broadband — from Management column.
digital.catSource: Secondary and Higher Secondary schools dominate digital infrastructure. Primary and Upper Primary schools face a digital divide that limits technology-enabled teaching at critical learning stages.
digital.catNote: Secondary schools have the highest digital infrastructure coverage. Foundational & Prep schools lag significantly — the digital divide starts at primary level.

insight1.label: Schools with Internet Connectivity · 2020-21
insight1.note: 25%. 11.4 L schools have no internet — impacting digital learning for millions

insight2.label: Avg Computers per Equipped School · 2020-21
insight2.note: AVG(. Total computers across UDISE+ = 5.26M. Computer equity across school types shows significant disparity

header.label: Health & Hygiene
header.headline: Medical checkups, handwashing, safe water — student wellbeing · 2020-21
header.sub: Health_Hygiene_infra_overall · 2020-21: 15.1 L schools. Medical checkup: 50%, Handwash at meals: 84%

cards.label: Medical Checkup
cards.note: 50%. Govt: 56%, Private: 35%. 13.0 Cr students in schools without medical checkup

cards.label: Handwash (Meals)
cards.note: 84%. Govt: 86%, Private: 80%. 3.5 Cr students in schools without handwash

cards.label: Handwash (Toilet)
cards.note: 79%. Govt: 77%, Private: 85%. 4.1 Cr students in schools without handwash

cards.label: Water Tested
cards.note: 30%. Govt: 27%, Private: 37%. 15.6 Cr students in schools without water tested

health.mgmtSource: Health and hygiene infrastructure is strongest in Central Govt schools. Hand-washing facilities show the widest gap — critical for preventive health in rural government schools.
health.mgmtNote: Central Govt and Private schools lead on all hygiene indicators. Government schools show largest gap on water testing — from Management column.
health.catSource: Health facility coverage improves steadily from Foundational to Secondary level. Medical checkup facilities are weakest at Preparatory stage — a critical gap for early health intervention.
health.catNote: Secondary schools show highest health & hygiene coverage. Foundational & Prep schools — serving youngest students — have the most critical gaps.

insight1.label: Schools Without Medical Checkup · 2020-21
insight1.note: = 7.5 L. Annual medical checkup is mandatory under NEP — yet 50% of schools don't conduct it. Children's early health detection depends on this

insight2.label: Schools Meeting All 4 Health Indicators · 2020-21
insight2.note: 16%. 12.6 L schools fall short on at least one health/hygiene requirement

header.label: Green & Sustainable
header.headline: Safe water, sanitation, sustainability — a greener school system · 2020-21
header.sub: Green_sustainable_infra_overall · 2020-21: 15.1 L schools. Water purifier: 26%, Girls toilet functional: 92%

cards.label: Water Purifier
cards.note: 26%. Govt: 20%, Private: 43%. 15.5 Cr students in schools without water purifier

cards.label: Water Tested
cards.note: 30%. Govt: 27%, Private: 37%. 15.6 Cr students in schools without water tested

cards.label: Handwash at Toilet
cards.note: 79%. Govt: 77%, Private: 85%. 4.1 Cr students in schools without handwash at toilet

cards.label: Girls Toilet Functional
cards.note: 92%. Govt: 92%, Private: 97%. 1.2 Cr students in schools without girls toilet functional

green.mgmtSource: Green infrastructure adoption is highest in urban private schools. Government schools in rural areas show the largest gaps in water purifiers and rainwater harvesting systems.
green.mgmtNote: Central Govt schools lead on water purifier and water testing. Government schools lag significantly on functional girls toilets. From Management column.
green.catSource: Green and sustainable infrastructure is most prevalent in Secondary schools. Foundational schools show the lowest adoption of eco-friendly facilities despite serving the most students.
green.catNote: Secondary schools have the highest sustainable infrastructure coverage. Functional girls' toilets are critical for girl retention — lowest in Foundational & Prep.

insight1.label: Schools with Functional Girls Toilet · 2020-21
insight1.note: 92%. Functional girls' toilets are directly linked to girl enrollment and retention. 1.2 Cr students in schools with non-functional girls' toilets
insight1.delta_label: Students in schools without functional girls toilet

insight2.label: Schools with Water Purifier · 2020-21
insight2.note: 26%. Safe drinking water through purification is an SDG6 target for schools. 11.1 L schools still rely on untreated water sources

cwsn.catSource: Secondary schools lead on CWSN infrastructure compliance. Foundational schools, which serve the youngest CWSN children, have the lowest ramp and accessible toilet coverage.
cwsn.catNote: Secondary schools have the highest CWSN facility coverage. Foundational & Prep schools — where many younger CWSN students study — have the least.

insight1.label: Schools with Both Ramps & Handrails · 2023-24
insight1.note: 52%. Full physical access requires both. 7.0 L schools lack at least one

insight2.label: Schools with No Ramp · 2023-24
insight2.note: Ramps are the most basic physical access requirement — yet 23% of schools still lack them, locking out wheelchair users

cards.label: Internet
cards.note: 54%. Govt: 46%, Private: 74%

cards.note: 57%. Total computers in system: 8.7M. Avg 10.4 computers per equipped school

cards.label: Broadband
cards.note: 12%. Govt: 5%, Private: 28%

cards.label: Comp Lab (≥5 PCs)
cards.note: 57%. Avg computers per equipped school = 10.4. 19.2 Cr

digital.mgmtSource: Digital infrastructure adoption is led by Government Aided schools for internet connectivity. Private Unaided schools lead on computer availability but lag on broadband quality.
digital.mgmtNote: Central Govt and Private Unaided schools lead on all digital indicators. Government schools show largest gap on broadband — from Management column.
digital.catSource: Secondary and Higher Secondary schools dominate digital infrastructure. Primary and Upper Primary schools face a digital divide that limits technology-enabled teaching at critical learning stages.
digital.catNote: Secondary schools have the highest digital infrastructure coverage. Foundational & Prep schools lag significantly — the digital divide starts at primary level.

insight1.label: Schools with Internet Connectivity · 2023-24
insight1.note: 54%. 6.8 L schools have no internet — impacting digital learning for millions

insight2.label: Avg Computers per Equipped School · 2023-24
insight2.note: AVG(. Total computers across UDISE+ = 8.72M. Computer equity across school types shows significant disparity

header.label: Health & Hygiene
header.headline: Medical checkups, handwashing, safe water — student wellbeing · 2023-24
header.sub: Health_Hygiene_infra_overall · 2023-24: 14.7 L schools. Medical checkup: 75%, Handwash at meals: 89%

cards.label: Medical Checkup
cards.note: 75%. Govt: 81%, Private: 59%. 6.2 Cr students in schools without medical checkup

cards.label: Handwash (Meals)
cards.note: 89%. Govt: 91%, Private: 88%. 2.0 Cr students in schools without handwash

cards.label: Handwash (Toilet)
cards.note: 89%. Govt: 88%, Private: 93%. 1.8 Cr students in schools without handwash

cards.label: Water Tested
cards.note: 43%. Govt: 39%, Private: 51%. 11.2 Cr students in schools without water tested

health.mgmtSource: Health and hygiene infrastructure is strongest in Central Govt schools. Hand-washing facilities show the widest gap — critical for preventive health in rural government schools.
health.mgmtNote: Central Govt and Private schools lead on all hygiene indicators. Government schools show largest gap on water testing — from Management column.
health.catSource: Health facility coverage improves steadily from Foundational to Secondary level. Medical checkup facilities are weakest at Preparatory stage — a critical gap for early health intervention.
health.catNote: Secondary schools show highest health & hygiene coverage. Foundational & Prep schools — serving youngest students — have the most critical gaps.

insight1.label: Schools Without Medical Checkup · 2023-24
insight1.note: = 3.7 L. Annual medical checkup is mandatory under NEP — yet 25% of schools don't conduct it. Children's early health detection depends on this

insight2.label: Schools Meeting All 4 Health Indicators · 2023-24
insight2.note: 34%. 9.7 L schools fall short on at least one health/hygiene requirement

cards.label: Water Purifier
cards.note: 30%. Govt: 22%, Private: 51%. 12.9 Cr students in schools without water purifier

cards.label: Water Tested
cards.note: 43%. Govt: 39%, Private: 51%. 11.2 Cr students in schools without water tested

cards.note: 89%. Govt: 88%, Private: 93%. 1.8 Cr students in schools without handwash at toilet

cards.label: Girls Toilet Functional
cards.note: 92%. Govt: 92%, Private: 94%. 1.3 Cr students in schools without girls toilet functional

green.mgmtSource: Green infrastructure adoption is highest in urban private schools. Government schools in rural areas show the largest gaps in water purifiers and rainwater harvesting systems.
green.mgmtNote: Central Govt schools lead on water purifier and water testing. Government schools lag significantly on functional girls toilets. From Management column.
green.catSource: Green and sustainable infrastructure is most prevalent in Secondary schools. Foundational schools show the lowest adoption of eco-friendly facilities despite serving the most students.
green.catNote: Secondary schools have the highest sustainable infrastructure coverage. Functional girls' toilets are critical for girl retention — lowest in Foundational & Prep.

insight1.label: Schools with Functional Girls Toilet · 2023-24
insight1.note: 92%. Functional girls' toilets are directly linked to girl enrollment and retention. 1.3 Cr students in schools with non-functional girls' toilets
insight1.delta_label: Students in schools without functional girls toilet

insight2.label: Schools with Water Purifier · 2023-24
insight2.note: 30%. Safe drinking water through purification is an SDG6 target for schools. 10.4 L schools still rely on untreated water sources

header.label: Basic Infrastructure
header.headline: Power, water, toilets — the basics that aren't basic yet · 2024-25
header.sub: 14.7 L schools. Electricity: 92%, Drinking Water: 99%, Toilet: 99%

cards.label: Electricity
cards.note: 92% of schools have electricity. Govt: 92%, Private: 92%. 0.8 Cr. students study in schools without electricity.

cards.label: Drinking Water
cards.note: 99% of schools have drinking water. Govt: 99%, Private: 99%. 0.1 Cr. students in schools without drinking water.

cards.label: Toilet
cards.note: 99% of schools have functional toilets. Govt: 99%, Private: 99%. 0.1 Cr. students in schools without toilets.

cards.label: Tinkering Lab
cards.note: 2% of schools have a tinkering lab. Govt: 1%, Private: 6%. 2.2 Cr. students have access to tinkering facilities.

basic.mgmtSource: Central Govt schools lead all basic indicators. Government schools lag Private Unaided by ~11pts on electricity access — a key equity gap to close.
basic.mgmtNote: Central Govt schools lead all four indicators. Government schools lag Private Unaided by ~11pts on electricity. From Management column in basic_infra_overall.
basic.catSource: Secondary schools have the highest basic infrastructure coverage. Foundational and Preparatory stages — serving the largest enrollment — show persistent gaps in electricity and tinkering labs.
basic.catNote: Foundational & Prep schools have the lowest coverage on every indicator — they serve the youngest and most numerous students.

insight1.label: Students in Schools Without a Toilet · 2024-25
insight1.note: total_students. Largest student-impact gap of any basic indicator. 1% of schools lack a toilet — concentrated in Foundational & Prep, government-managed schools

insight2.label: Schools with All 3 Basic Facilities · 2024-25
insight2.note: 91%. 9% of schools — 1.3 L — are missing at least one basic facility
insight2.delta_label: Schools missing at least one basic facility
